observations.recommendation:
1.10 We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer submit to the Municipal
Accountant the copies of RAAO and the SAAOB to facilitate the preparation
and submission of the SCBAA for CYs 2022, 2023, and 2024.

1.11 We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive enforce Section 122
of P.D. 1445 on the suspension of the salary of the Municipal Budget Officer
until his submission of the SCBAA to the Municipal Accountant.

2.7 We recommended that:
5. The Municipal Accountant –
a. Prepare adjusting entries to correct the above-mentioned accounting
errors and deficiencies, for proper valuation of the PPE; and
b. Henceforth, ensure that transactions are properly recorded in the
books, in accordance with the prescribed accounting standards, rules,
and regulations.
6. The Municipal Engineer to submit the report on the status of the projects
implemented by the Municipality to the Municipal Accountant for ease of
recording of completed projects to their appropriate accounts;
7. The Local Chief Executive –
a. Create a Disposal and Appraisal Committee, which will facilitate the
disposal of the unserviceable properties;
b. Prepare the Appraisal Review Report on Property for Disposal
(ARRPD) showing the appraised values of the assets and submit a
copy of the same to the Audit Team for review;
c. Convene and agree on the mode of disposal of the items after
receiving the ARRPD duly reviewed by the audit team and
recommend the applicable mode of disposal for the Local Chief
Executive’s approval and submit these documents to the Audit Team
for evaluation and action thereon prior to the disposal of
unserviceable items; and
d. Take up the necessary steps and procedures for the identified non-
existent/missing properties set forth in COA Circular 2020-006
8. The OIC-GSO and Inventory Committee –
a. Conduct reconciliation of the PPE accounts between the accounting
and property records; and
b. Inform the Municipal Accountant of the result of the physical count
of PPE, to update their respective records and facilitate the
preparation of reports.

2.8 We further reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that management
conduct a One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances, following the
guidelines and procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated
January 31, 2020, to address the problem of reconciliation of PPE items in the
accounting and property records, which have become a perennial issue and
caused the non-establishment of the accuracy of the PPE balances presented
in the financial statements.

3.7 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw the necessary
adjusting journal entries for the noted errors after verification and supported
with documents. Henceforth, thoroughly review the accounts and observe the
proper recording of transactions in the books.

1.25 We recommended that:
1. The Municipal Accountant –
a. Prepare adjusting entries to correct the above-mentioned accounting
errors and deficiencies, for proper valuation of the PPE; and
b. Henceforth, ensure that transactions are properly recorded in the books,
in accordance with the prescribed accounting standards, rules, and
regulations.
2. The OIC-GSO –
a. Prepare and submit the RPCPPE;
b. Conduct reconciliation of the PPE accounts between the accounting and
property records; and
c. Prepare and maintain Property Cards.
3. The Chairperson of the Inventory Committee inform the Municipal
Accountant and the OIC-GSO of the result of the physical count of PPE,
to update their respective records and facilitate the preparation of reports.
1.26 We further reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that management
conduct a One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances, following the guidelines
and procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020,
to address the problem of reconciliation of PPE items in the accounting and
property records, which have become a perennial issue and caused the non-
establishment of the accuracy of the PPE balances presented in the financial
statements.

2.17 We recommended that:
1. The Municipal Accountant –
a. Record adjustments for the restoration of unreleased checks at the end
of the year to the cash in bank account, to fairly present the balances of
the Cash and Payable accounts at year-end;
b. Determine the cause of the difference between the book and bank
balance of the PHIC-1 Trust Fund account at the end of the year, and
draw the necessary adjustments to reconcile the cash in bank balances;
and
c. draw correcting journal entries for the erroneous recording in the Cash
– Local Treasury account, to faithfully represent the effects of
transactions.
2. The Municipal Treasurer –
a. Prepare a list of unreleased checks at year end, and furnish the
Municipal Accountant, to serve as her basis for drawing adjustments;
b. Require the payees to indicate in the check register their
names/signatures and specify the dates when checks were claimed from
the Treasurer’s Office for proper monitoring, and only release checks
to the payees or to their duly authorized representatives; and
c. Regularly conduct reconciliation between the cashbooks and
accounting records.

3.9 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw the necessary
adjusting entries for the noted accounting errors, to fairly present the accounts
in the financial statements.
3.10 We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer send written notices
to the payees with existing stale checks, informing them to present these checks
for replacement, to settle the Municipality’s liabilities.

4.8 We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer submit to the Municipal
Accountant the copies of RAAO and the SAAOB to facilitate the preparation
and submission of the SCBAA for CYs 2022 and 2023.

5.6 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant observe the proper
recording of transactions in the Trust Fund books, and the preparation of its
financial statements.

6.5 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary
adjusting journal entries to recognize the unliquidated Cash Advances under
the Confidential Fund.
6.6 We also recommended that subsequent releases from the Confidential Fund be
initially recorded as Advances to Special Disbursing Officer, and only consider
it as liquidated upon receipt of the copy of the transmittal letter of liquidation,
duly acknowledged by the ICFAU.

7.5 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the transactions in
the books using the proper accounts, as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-
009.

8.8 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw adjusting entries to
return to the GF the amount of the CO balance, to reflect the identified amount
of the unexpended MOOE balance to be transferred to the STF.
8.9 We also recommended that the OIC-MDRRM Officer:
a. Include the amount of the unexpended/unobligated returned from the TF
to the GF in the ensuing year’s LDRRMFIP, specifying the equipment that
will be purchased, to be integrated in the approved Annual Investment
Program; and
b. Keep abreast of the guidelines on the utilization, budgeting, and pertinent
accounting rules and regulations relative to the LDRRMF, this being an
integral part of his designation and for the successful implementation of
DRRM-related activities.

9.8 We recommended that the OIC-MDRRM Officer:
a. Make representation to the management to allocate enough funds from the
General Fund/regular appropriation to cover the other maintenance and
operating expenses of the MDRRM Office, to prevent charging of ineligible
expenses from the LDRRMF; and
b. Review transactions charged against the LDRRMF, whether they are
aligned to disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response,
rehabilitation and recovery purposes, in accordance with the NDDRMC,
DBM and DILG JMC 2013-1.
9.9 We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer stop certifying as to
existence of available appropriation from the LDRRMF for transactions
which are not chargeable against the said Fund.

10.10 We recommended that that management stop the realignment of funds from
the continuing appropriation or any capital outlay appropriation in the
LDRRMFIP, unless full implementation/completion of the PPAs or savings
therefrom are clearly identified and declared.
10.11 We also recommended that the OIC-MDRRM Officer keep abreast of the
guidelines on the utilization, budgeting, and pertinent accounting laws and
regulations relative to the LDRRMF, this being an integral part of his
designation and for the successful implementation of DRRM-related activities.

11.11 We recommended that management stop the realignment of funds without any
legal basis authorizing such fund transfers.
11.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer strictly monitor the
existence of available appropriations before certifying in the Certification on
Appropriations, Funds, and Obligations of Allotment, to limit the
disbursement of funds only up to the appropriated amounts in the
annual/supplemental budget.

12.7 We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal
Accountant diligently monitor the appropriations and obligations balances of
the Confidential Funds, and ensure that limits set in the Appropriation
Ordinance/Annual Budget are strictly adhered to, to avoid disallowances in
audit.

13.4 We recommended that that Management submit immediately the Liquidation
Reports and supporting documents to the ICFAU for the unliquidated cash
advances under the Confidential Fund in CYs 2022 and 2023.
13.5 We also recommended that the SDO refrain from making additional cash
advances from the Confidential Fund until all outstanding cash advances are
fully liquidated to the ICFAU.

14.8 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the
SDO, submit the identified lacking documents for the CY 2023 Confidential
Fund cash advances, as follows:
a. Certification by the HoA using the prescribed format;
b. Certified copy of the transmittal letter of the LR of the previous cash
advances duly stamped “received” by the ICFAU, COA and certification
of the Accountant that the SDO has no unliquidated CF/IF cash advances;
c. Certified copy of the minutes of the meeting evidencing the 2/3 votes of the
Local Peace and Order Council approving the POP and the release of the
CF; and
d. Certification from the concerned PNP Chief in the locality highlighting the
peace and order situation in the locality and supporting the need to release
and use the CF.
14.9 Henceforth, ensure the timely submission of the Disbursement Vouchers and
their supporting documents to the COA Auditor, strictly following the
guidelines set forth in the COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No.
2015-01.

15.7 We recommended that Management adopt the automated payroll system for
the payment of salaries and wages of officials, employees, and job order
personnel, considering the availability of an ATM within the vicinity of the
Municipality.

16.9 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer stop the encashment of checks
out of the cash collections under his custody.

17.9 We recommended that the MPDO ensure that PPAs are not presented in lump
sum appropriations and not described in generic terms, but are instead
itemized, containing sufficient information as to the specific project titles and
locations, costs, and schedule of implementation.
17.10 We also recommended that management strive for the optimal utilization of
the 20% DF, towards the implementation of PPAs whose nature and purposes
are aligned to the achievement of the desired socio-economic and
environmental outcomes of the LGU.

18.9 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer refrain from making cash
advances for labor payroll before the actual rendition/completion of the labor
works and undertake immediate liquidation of the said cash advances after
disbursements, with complete documentation.
18.10 We also recommended that the BAC strictly follow the proper procurement
procedures in the implementation of projects.

19.9 We recommended that the LGU obtain a certification from the identified
beneficiaries, showing the same signatures as presented in their respective
valid IDs, to prove that they have indeed received the stipend for the 1st
Semester of CY 2023.
19.10 Henceforth, we recommended that the SDO and the focal person of the social
pension program:
a. Stop the practice of making the social pension recipients sign the
payrolls prior to the pay-out and the actual receipt of cash;
b. Verify the validity of the signatures on the payroll and that appearing
on the presented valid ID; and
c. Formulate and implement a pay-out system that will mitigate the risks
inherent to carrying huge amounts of cash on the field, without
sacrificing the established controls on cash pay-outs.

20.7 We recommended that that the LGU fully comply with the stipulations in the
Memoranda of Agreement executed with the source agencies for all funds
received for the implementation of projects/programs/activities, and
undertake liquidation thereof within the agreed period.

21.5 We recommended that the BAC Secretariat submit to the COA Audit Team
the required copies of the contracts and their supporting documents within
five days from the execution of the contract, in compliance with Section 3.1.1
of COA Circular No. 2009-001.

1.23 We recommended that:
1. The Municipal Accountant –
a. Exert effort to trace back details of PPE acquisitions, recompute the
accumulated depreciation of the CY 2022 reported balance of depreciable
assets, on a per item basis, using the formula for straight line method of
depreciation, and effect the necessary adjustments after determination of
the correct amount, for the proper valuation of the PPE;
b. Reclassify the intangible assets to the proper account; and
c. Maintain PPE ledger cards/subsidiary records for items of PPE and
coordinate with the General Services Officer (GSO) and Inventory
Committee for the identification of recorded assets that were not
accounted in the RPCPPE. Undertake reconciliation of records, to
accurately and reliably present the PPE accounts in the financial
statements.
2. The GSO –
a. Compare existing records with the Municipal Accountant for
tracing/identification of PPE which were not accounted in the
RPCPPE, and reconcile PPE items and their balances between the
accounting and property records; and
b. Prepare and maintain Property Cards.
3. The Chairperson of the Inventory Committee –
a. Include real properties in the physical count of PPE; and
b. Communicate to the Municipal Accountant and GSO the result of the
physical count of PPE to update their respective records.
1.24 We further recommended that management conduct a One-Time Cleansing of PPE
Account Balances, following the guidelines and procedures set forth in COA Circular
No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, to address the problem of unidentified PPE
items in the accounting records and discrepancies in PPE account balances, which
have become a perennial issue and caused the non-establishment of the accuracy of
the PPE balances presented in the financial statements.

2.11 We recommended that:
1. The Municipal Accountant record adjustments for the reversion of stale
checks and restoration of unreleased checks at the end of the year to the
cash account, to fairly present the balances of the Cash and Payable
accounts at year end.
2. The Municipal Treasurer –
a. Send written notices to the payees at least one month before checks
become stale;
b. Maintain a logbook for the release of checks, for proper monitoring; and
c. Prepare a list of unreleased checks at year end, and furnish the
Municipal Accountant, to serve as her basis for drawing adjustments.

3.8 We recommended that:
1. The Municipal Treasurer –
a. Immediately cause the transfer of funds due to the TF from the GF;
b. Always monitor the cash in bank balances for all Funds; and
c. Stop certifying as to the availability of funds, and refrain from drawing
checks, if such funds intended for the purpose are not yet transferred
to its proper bank account.
2. The Municipal Accountant –
a. Prepare the bank reconciliation statements monthly, in order to
regularly monitor the cash in bank balances and regulate
disbursements; and
b. Make the necessary adjustments in the books, upon verification of the
deficiencies noted.

4.7 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant revisit the Trust Fund books
to determine the transactions that brought about the Government Equity
balance, and effect the necessary adjustments. Henceforth, observe the proper
recording of transactions in the Trust Fund books, and the preparation of its
financial statements.

5.8 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the accrued interest
and the passed-on GRT incurred for December 15 to 31, 2022 from the loan at
LBP, and recognize the borrowing costs at the end of the year to fairly present
the balances of the accounts in the financial statements.

6.6 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the CY 2022 SCBAA
to COA, and henceforth, observe the timely submission of the complete set of
financial statements for audit.

7.6 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare a duly certified list
showing the taxpayers and amounts due and collectible and submit the same
to the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year, to serve as the basis
for setting up the RPT and SET Receivables in the books.

8.5 We recommended that the GSO submit the requested copies of AIRs and
delivery receipts for the month of December 2022.
8.6 We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit to the audit
team the supporting documents for the recorded Accounts Payable at year
end, to verify the accuracy and validity of the claims.

9.9 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
a. Demand from the supplier the issuance of the proper Sales Invoices for the
above noted transactions, to establish legitimacy of the settlement of
payables and receipt of payments by the supplier; and
b. Stop accepting sales invoices issued by an establishment other than the
payee in the checks, and ensure that all claimed/payments made by the
Municipality are evidenced by proper and valid sales invoices or official
receipts from suppliers which are required to issue the same, and not
merely by RERs.

10.9 We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer strictly implement the
Municipality’s vaccination process flow; ensure that persons inoculated
during the day are guided to the post monitoring station for inclusion in the
Post Vaccination Monitoring List; and properly inform the vaccinees of the
purpose and importance of such monitoring list, for the effective monitoring
of the vaccine recipients for any AEFI.

11.8 We recommended that the BAC keep abreast of the various issuances of the
DOH, relative to the procurements of COVID-19 essential medicines and
medical devices, and ensure compliance with the updated SRPs, so that the
most advantageous prevailing price is availed of by the Municipality.

12.14 We recommended that the BAC properly plan and monitor the Municipality’s
procurements and ensure that quantities procured are within the average
three-month requirement. We further recommended that the provisions on
the general policy on competitive bidding, and the prohibition of splitting of
requisitions/contracts, be strictly adhered to.

13.6 We recommended that the MTO refrain from collecting rentals not covered
by appropriate ordinances or approved Revenue Code of the Municipality.
13.7 We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) or his
representative make a representation to the Sangguniang Bayan for
amendments or additional ordinances for new sources of income not yet
covered in the Municipality’s Revenue Code, for due process.

14.8 We recommended that the LCE:
a. Ensure that clear policies and guidelines about the System and proper
training be provided to the BPLO and MTO personnel to prevent the
commission of errors; and
b. Disseminate adequate information to the Taxpayers on the requirements
to be submitted to facilitate the accurate computation of their taxes.

14.9 We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and his Collectors
input to the System the correct tax rates on businesses as prescribed in the
Revenue Code of the Municipality.

15.10 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
a. Collect the unpaid rentals and surcharges;
b. Adequately inform and update the collectors of the provisions in the
Revenue Code of the Municipality of Oras and relevant SB Resolutions, or
conduct orientation/training, if necessary; and
c. Monitor the payments made by the stall owner/taxpayers and ensure that
proper amounts due are being collected from them.

16.8 We recommended that the BAC submit the original approved lease contract
for the purchase and rental of agricultural machines and equipment under the
fund received from the DTI to the Office of the Auditor.

16.9 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant thoroughly check the
completeness of supporting documents of all claims and ensure that all
documentary requirements have been complied with before processing
disbursement vouchers, to avoid suspensions during the audit.

17.23 We recommended that the Municipal Engineer:
a. Faithfully report the status of projects and their actual percentage of
completion as at a given period, in conformance with the government’s
directive of transparency of and accountability in the use of public funds;
b. Adequately plan, assess and evaluate program of works/variation orders,
and ensure that related cost estimates are properly prepared, and
compliant with the provisions in Annex E of RIRR of RA No. 9184;
c. Keep the billboard/tarpaulin of construction projects updated, and reflect
all the progress and revisions, if any, to properly inform and update the
public, for transparency; and
d. Submit data to the Municipal Accountant, for the computation and
enforcement of liquidated damages for projects with delayed completion.

18.7 We recommended that the BAC:
a. Submit an explanation or justification for resorting to small value
procurement instead of through competitive bidding for the procurement
of goods for stockpile;
b. Consolidate the requisitions of goods of similar nature and purpose, and
have them procured through public bidding so the Municipality could take
advantage of lower prices through bulk purchase; and
c. Stop splitting the requisitions/purchase orders, and adhere to the general
policy on competitive bidding.

19.5 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the subject DVs and
supporting documents to the audit team, relative to LDRRM Fund
disbursements, for audit and verification.

20.6 We recommended that:
1. LCE executes Job Order contracts between the Municipality and the hired
JOs, specifying therein the following: wage rate, work duration,
functions/duties, that there is no employer-employee relationship between
the parties, and that work rendered is not considered as government
service;
2. The JO personnel submit authenticated copies of the logbook/biometric
printouts, and Accomplishment Reports certified correct by the officials
who have direct supervision over their work, specifying in detail the work
accomplishments for the particular period;
3. The HRMO/officials concerned/direct supervisors ensure that the hired
JOs are faithfully reporting their log-in and log-out entries in their DTRs,
and are performing their respective work assignment/duties, as indicated
in their job order contracts; and
4. The Municipal Accountant ensure the completeness of supporting
documents before claims are processed.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries for the transfer of the above PPEs to its appropriate accounts and to recognize depreciation on the said PPE accounts after determining total costs attributable to the asset and the time when the assets were put into use by management.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented
observations.reason: The determination of the detailed costing of cabinets and tables is still ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the department/offices or end-user units to meticulously support all the requirements and cost estimates of the different PPAs as embodied in the AIP, taking into consideration the procurement of development projects that partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures and will contribute to the attainment of the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU and refrain charging from the fund expenditure items not allowed under the regulation.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented
observations.reason: The determination of the detailed costing of the different PPAs as embodied in the AIP, there were still charges against the 20% Development Fund not allowed under the pertinent regulation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to the Management to conduct a complete physical inventory count of all municipal properties including Infrastructure Assets owned by the Municipality and submit a complete RPCPPE and RPCRNS and immediately reconcile it with the balance of the Property, Plant and Equipment account to permit a fair presentation of the properties owned by the municipality. We further recommended to the GSO to regularly update and submit the respective property cards for easy monitoring of the account.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented
observations.reason: Some of the PPEs do not have yet their respective property PPE cards.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MPDC, Municipal Assessor, Municipal Engineer, Municipal Treasurer, GSO to coordinate in the identification, inventory, measurement, recording and maintenance of records and reports for local roads and road components under the jurisdiction of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented
observations.reason: Partially identified some of the local road networks however the measurement and identification of the components and assessed value were not yet accomplished.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the responsible persons to exert extra effort in the timely implementation of the projects and make necessary arrangements, if necessary in order to fully comply with the guidelines set by the sourced agency to ensure that the funds will be utilized for its intended purpose. We also recommended for the immediate return to its source agency the unutilized balance of the projects completed in adherence to COA Circular No. 94-013.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented
observations.reason: Despite implementation of the projects for which the funds were intended for, unused funds are yet to be returned to the source agencies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, the Budget Officer and other concerned municipal officials to prepare a detailed AIP for the 20% Development Fund indicating specific project description and the corresponding amount and see to it that the fund will be utilized to finance LGU’s priority development projects that will contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes and will partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented
observations.reason: Some of the PPAs identified in the AIP were still in generic project description.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the GAD Focal Point System to: a) spearhead the setting up and maintenance of GAD Database by coordinating with DILG Field Operation’s Officer and other concerned LGU officials. Further, we recommended the GAD Focal Point System to institutionalize the GAD database with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation; b) undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues and ensure that the programs, projects and activities are responsive to the said issues, and c) revisit Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 as amended by JMC 2016-01, which identifies the data requirements for particular gender issues.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented
observations.reason: The updates and encoding of GAD Database is still ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
1.12 We recommended that the municipal officials concerned classify appropriately the
assets procured, conduct procedures that will reconcile all its records for the
Property, Plant and Equipment, and compute correctly the depreciation for its
depreciable assets to assure the users of the financial statements on its existence,
accuracy and reliability and in order to achieve the fair presentation of the
account in the financial statements.

2.4 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant correctly record the cost of the
items purchased to its proper account classification in order not to overstate the assets
of the municipality.

3.18 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant
and the Municipal Treasurer to:
a. Submit regularly monthly Report of Collections and Deposits, Report of
Accountability for Accountable Forms With and Without Money Value to support
the submitted issued and cancelled ORs to the Municipal Accountant and to the
Audit Team and to monitor the correct ORs requisitioned, issued and balance on
hand.
b. Have a regular reconciliation between Collectors’, Municipal Treasurer and the
Municipal Accountant’s records in the recording of collections and deposits and
other reconciling items so that appropriate adjustments could be made on a
monthly basis and to substantiate the reported Cash in Local Treasury account in
the financial statement.
c. Prepare the necessary adjusting entries for any identified reconciling items so that
the reported Cash in Local Treasury account balance of ₱2,027,587.26 at year end
will be corrected.

4.18 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant
and the Municipal Treasurer to:
a. Prepare a corrected bank reconciliation statement for all eleven (11) bank
accounts by recording all the reconciling items and effect any adjustments to the
accounts accordingly and submit the subsidiary and general ledger for Cash in
Bank, Local Currency Current Account of the General Fund to substantiate the
reported account in the financial statement.
b. Have a regular reconciliation of their records, monitor cancelled, outstanding,
unreleased and stale checks, in the recording of collection and deposits and
disbursements and other reconciling items so that appropriate adjustments could
be made on a monthly basis.

5.7 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant
to:
a. Prepare necessary adjusting entries in the General Fund book for the transaction
pertaining to the unpaid terminal leave pay in order that the accounts involved
can be fairly presented in the FS; and
b. Always observe the proper classification of accounts by strictly adhering to the
prescribed Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs.

6.8 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting entries that will derecognize
the ₱181,410.70 balance of the Property and Equipment for Distribution
account and correct the Government Equity balance. Likewise, enter each
transaction to its appropriate account in compliance with the Revised
Chart of Accounts for LGUs.
b. GSO to consolidate RIS using the SSMI and furnish the Municipal
Accountant a copy thereof for recording.

7.7 We recommended that LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to make timely
request of the schedule of amortization from the lending institution in order to classify
and present the current and non-current portion of the Loans Payable-Domestic
account as prescribed by IPSAS 1 and to provide more relevant data to the users of
the FS.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a corrected bank reconciliation statement by reflecting all reconciling items and draw adjustments to the affected accounts accordingly. Further, we recommended that a coordination should be made between the Accounting and Treasurer’s Office to monitor checks that are cancelled, cancelled, and unreleased so that appropriate adjustments could be made on a timely manner.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE require the General Service Officer to conduct physical inventory of all remaining items of goods, supplies, materials including drugs and medicines and medical supplies and equipment in the custody of responsible officials and determine unused or undistributed items. Likewise, require the submission of Stock Cards, Requisition Issue Slip, Inventory Custodian Slip, and such other records/reports supporting issuance of supplies and materials.
Further, require the Municipal Accountant to recognize in the books any undistributed/unissued inventory items by preparing a Journal Entry Voucher to record the inventory items in the corresponding inventory ledger card.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entries to take up the depreciation of the related PPEs for the current year as provided in the above cited regulations. We further recommended that a Lapsing Schedule for Depreciation be prepared to serve as a guide in the computation of the annual depreciation.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive cause the immediate liquidation of the cash advances granted to LGU officials & employees to determine the propriety, legality, and reliability of the transaction. We further recommended to strictly adhere to laws, rules and regulations in the grant, utilization, and liquidation of cash advances as provided in COA Circular No. 97-002, to ensure efficient and effective management of government funds.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to record the receipt of the funds in the appropriate “Trust Liability-DRRMF” account as provided in COA Circular No. 2014-02.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the submission of the required supporting documents to establish validity of the disbursements taken out of the BGCM funds.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Budget Officer to prepare a Registry of Appropriation, Obligations, and Disbursements for each PPA under the BGCM with the required data such as Obligation Number, Date, Check Number, and other details which could be traced to the budget authorization (Obligation Request) attached to the DV to identify the charges incurred against the fund and in support to the Report on the Utilization of the Fund.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the submission of the supplemental AIP supported with LDC Resolution and approved by the local sanggunian thru a resolution passed by the Sanggunian Bayan and approved by the Local Chief Executive.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and the Municipal Engineer to cause proper monitoring and evaluation of project implementation and accomplishment to ensure that government funds are optimally utilized and benefits are received by intended beneficiaries.
Prospectively, report on the status of the projects undertaken by the recipient agencies, supported with accomplishment report and other documents should be submitted to COA for evaluation.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and the Budget Officer to strictly monitor the charges against the LDRRMF by maintaining a separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) for QRF, MOOE and CO and certify only items of expenditures that are within the appropriated amounts based on the duly approved LDRRMFIP.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Budget Officer to strictly monitor the charges against the LDRRMF by maintaining a separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) for QRF, MOOE and CO and certify only items of expenditures that are within the appropriated amounts based on the duly approved LDRRMFIP.
observations.reason: The Budget Officer did not prepare a separate RAAO for QRF, MOOE and CO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a corrected bank reconciliation statement by reflecting all reconciling items and draw adjustments to the affected accounts accordingly. Further, we recommended that a coordination should be made between the Accounting and Treasurer’s Office to monitor checks that are stale, cancelled, and unreleased so that appropriate adjustments could be made on a timely manner.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal General Service Officer to conduct physical inventory of all remaining items of goods, supplies, materials including drugs and medicines and medical supplies and equipment in the custody of responsible officials and determine unused or undistributed items. Likewise, require the submission of Stock Cards, Requisition Issue Slip, Inventory Custodian Slip, and such other records/reports supporting issuance of supplies and materials.

Further, we recommended to require the Municipal Accountant to recognize in the books any undistributed/unissued inventory items by preparing a Journal Entry Voucher to record the inventory items in the corresponding inventory ledger card.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to   prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries for the transfer of the above PPEs to its appropriate accounts and to recognize depreciation on the said PPE accounts after determining total costs attributable to the asset and the time when the assets were put into use by management.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entries to take up the depreciation of the related PPEs for the current year as provided in the above cited regulations. We further recommended that a Lapsing Schedule for Depreciation be prepared to serve as a guide in the computation of the annual depreciation.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive cause the immediate liquidation of the cash advances granted to LGU officials & employees to determine the propriety, legality, and reliability of the transaction.  We further recommended to strictly adhere to laws, rules and regulations in the grant, utilization, and liquidation of cash advances as provided in COA Circular No. 97-002, to ensure efficient and effective management of government funds.

We recommended that the LCE, require the department/offices or end-user units to meticulously and judiciously plan all procurement and prepare their PPMPs to support all the requirements and cost estimates of the different PPAs as embodied in the AIP, taking into consideration the procurement of development projects that partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures and  will contribute to the attainment of the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU and refrain charging from the fund expenditure items not allowed under the regulation.

We recommended that the LCE require the preparation of Liquidation Report or Report on the Utilization of each trust fund received and submit the same to the source agency, copy furnished the COA.

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to record the receipt of the funds in the appropriate “Trust Liability- DRRMF” account as provided in COA Circular No. 2014-02.

We recommended that the LCE require the submission of the required supporting documents identified and discussed above and the legal basis for the grant of hazard/incentive pay to establish validity of the disbursements taken out of the BGCM funds.

We recommended to submit justification why the LGU failed to create a special account for the BGCM. Further, require the Budget Officer to prepare a Registry of Appropriation, Obligations, and Disbursements for each PPA under the BGCM with the required data such as Obligation Number, Date, Check Number, and other details which could be traced to the budget authorization (Obligation Request) attached to the DV to identify the charges incurred against the fund and in support to the Report on the Utilization of the Fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the submission of the supplemental AIP supported with LDC Resolution and approved by the local sanggunian thru a resolution passed by the Sanggunian Bayan and approved by the Local Chief Executive.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to a) prepare a JEV to record the reversion to CIB-LCCA and recognition of payable accounts for all stale checks that remained outstanding, b) submit schedule and supporting documents for the cancellation of stale checks of P36,775.64 and an accounting of P14,660.00  outstanding stale checks not reflected in the 2019 bank reconciliation statement, c) prepare and submit bank reconciliation statement regularly  so that the errors could be determined and corrected immediately.
observations.reason:
The Accountant has prepared a JEV for the reversion of stale checks to CIB-LCCA and recording of the corresponding Accounts Payable 

A monthly Bank Reconciliation Statement is still not submitted regularly. 

This is reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to the Management to conduct a complete physical inventory count of all municipal properties including Infrastructure Assets owned by the Municipality and submit a complete RPCPPE and RPCRNS and immediately reconcile it with the balance of the Property, Plant and Equipment account to permit a fair presentation of the properties owned by the municipality. We further recommended to the GSO to regularly update and submit the PPE cards for easy monitoring of the account.
observations.reason:
The management was not able to conduct complete physical inventory of all municipal properties including Infrastructure Assets due to COVID-19 restrictions.

The GSO still does not prepare and submit the PPE Cards.

This is reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MPDC, Municipal Assessor, Municipal Engineer, Municipal Treasurer, GSO and Municipal Accountant to coordinate in the identification, inventory, measurement, recording and maintenance of records and reports for local roads and road components under the jurisdiction of the LGU.
observations.reason:
The LGU has already started in the listing and identification of Local Road Networks but due to the COVID-19 pandemic no physical inventory of Local Road Networks was conducted.

This has been reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the responsible persons to exert extra effort in the timely implementation of the projects and make necessary arrangements, if necessary in order to fully comply with the guidelines set by the sourced agency to ensure that the funds will be utilized for its intended purpose. We also recommended for the immediate return to its source agency the unutilized balance of the projects completed in adherence to COA Circular No. 94-013.
observations.reason:
There are still projects not implemented, specially BFAR-BuB projects with total project cost of P1,735,000. The POWs of these projects were revised  and submitted to BFAR, agency still awaiting for the approval. 

While the unutilized balance from finished projects amounting P26,380.04.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and Municipal Engineer to cause proper monitoring and evaluation of project implementation and accomplishment to ensure that government funds are optimally utilized and benefits are received by the intended beneficiaries. Prospectively, report on the status of the projects undertaken by the recipient agencies, supported with accomplishment report and other documents should be submitted to COA for evaluation.
observations.reason: The LGU has already conducted the monitoring and evaluation for projects given to the barangays and the MPDO has made the corresponding Accomplishment Report. However,

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Human Resource Officer to revisit its guidelines in the hiring and management of Job Order/Casual personnel, maintain and regularly update the records of LGU personnel together with their contract of service, DTRs and Accomplishment Report. It is further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer refrain from granting cash advances for payment of wages to job order and casual personnel before services are rendered and that the Municipal Accountant should only approve claims for wages with complete supporting documents.
observations.reason: DTRs, contracts of service  already submitted to COA

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, the Budget Officer and other concerned municipal officials to prepare a detailed AIP for the 20% Development Fund indicating specific project description and the corresponding amount and see to it that the fund will be utilized to finance to LGU’s priority development projects that will contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes and will partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures.
observations.reason:
The attached document to support the AIP has no specific amount for each project description.  

This is reiterated in this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to reclassify the granting of livelihood assistance to NGOs/POs treated as Donations into Due from Non-Government Organizations/People’s Organizations account to properly monitor the implementation of the projects. 
We also recommended to require the recipients to prepare and submit a Fund Utilization Report/Accomplishment Report prior to granting additional livelihood assistance or taking up adjustments in the book to ensure that the fund is optimally utilized.
observations.reason: The reason why the granting of livelihood assistance to NGOs/POs were treated as Donations and not Due from Non-Government Organizations/People’s Organizations is because, the assistance given to them were not cash but supplies and materials.  Acknowledgment Receipts were duly signed by the recipients and Accomplishment Reports were made by the monitoring team for all livelihood assistance.  This livelihood monitoring team conducts regular monitoring of projects and makes recommendations and technical assistance to the organizations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Local Planning and Development Office spearheads the setting up and maintenance of GAD Database by coordinating with DILG Field Operation’s Officer and other concerned LGU officials. Further, we recommended the GAD Focal Point System to: a) institutionalize the GAD database with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation; b) undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues and ensure that the programs, projects and activities are responsive to the said issues, and c) revisit Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 as amended by JMC 2016-01, which identifies the data requirements for particular gender issues.
observations.reason: The management is still in the process of completing the encoding to the system of the GAD database. The GAD database is sex-disaggregated data which were derived from the administrative reports from local offices and from the barangay household profile.  It already includes data related to health, social welfare, senior citizen, and other social development related data.  In fact, we were able to utilize these data in the formulation of the LGU’s different plans such as CDP, ELA, CLUP, CDRA, and others.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the disbursement vouchers together with their required supporting documents as well as the cancelled checks and direct the person(s) involved to strictly adhere to the timely compliance of submission of the required documents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Accountant to a) prepare a JEV to record the reversion to CIB-LCCA and
recognition of payable accounts for all stale checks that remained outstanding, b)
submit schedule and supporting documents for the cancellation of stale checks of
P36,775.64 and an accounting of P14,660.00 outstanding state checks not
reflected in the 2019 bank reconciliation statement, c) prepare and submit bank
reconciliation statement regularly so that the errors could be determined and
corrected immediately.
We also recommended to direct the Municipal Treasurer to determine the
existence of outstanding checks nearing 6 months from the date of issue, on the
basis of bank reconciliation statement prepared by the Municipal Accountant
and send the payee a written notice of the existence of the checks at least one
month before the checks becomes stale (Section 59, Vol. 1, NGAS Manual for
LGUS).

We recommended to the Management to conduct a complete physical inventory
count of all municipal properties including Infrastructure Assets owned by the
Municipality and submit a complete RPCPPE and RPCRNS and immediately
reconcile it with the balance of the Property, Plant and Equipment account to permit
a fair presentation of the properties owned by the municipality. We further
recommended to the GSO to regularly update and submit the PPE cards for easy
monitoring of the account.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MPDC, Municipal
Assessor, Municipal Engineer, Municipal Treasurer, GSO and Municipal Accountant
to coordinate in the identification, inventory, measurement, recording and
maintenance of records and reports for local roads and road components under the
jurisdiction of the LGU.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant to fully disclose the
details of the Local Road Network in the Notes to the Financial Statement.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the responsible persons
to exert extra effort in the timely implementation of the projects and make
necessary arrangements, if necessary in order to fully comply with the guidelines
set by the sourced agency to ensure that the funds will be utilized for its intended
purpose. We also recommended for the immediate return to its source agency the
unutilized balance of the projects completed in adherence to COA Circular No.
94-013.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Planning
and Development Officer and Municipal Engineer to cause proper monitoring
and evaluation of project implementation and accomplishment to ensure that
government funds are optimally utilized and benefits are received by the intended
beneficiaries. Prospectively, report on the status of the projects undertaken by
the recipient agencies, supported with accomplishment report and other
documents should be submitted to COA for evaluation.
We also recommended that the LGU should require the concerned recipient
agency to submit Accomplishment Report for the assistance received before a
new one will be granted in adherence to COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Human Resource
Officer to revisit its guidelines in the hiring and management of Job Order/Casual
personnel, maintain and regularly update the records of LGU personnel together with
their contract of service, DTRs and Accomplishment Report. It is further
recommended that the Municipal Treasurer refrain from granting cash advances for
payment of wages to job order and casual personnel before services are rendered and
that the Municipal Accountant should only approve claims for wages with complete
supporting documents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Planning and Development Officer, the Budget Officer and other concerned municipal
officials to prepare a detailed AIP for the 20% Development Fund indicating specific
project description and the corresponding amount and see to it that the fund will be
utilized to finance to LGU’s priority development projects that will contribute to the
attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management
outcomes and will partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures.
We further recommended that the LCE require the implementing agencies to
submit accomplishment report for projects implemented out of the assistance
received from the municipality and Planning and Development Officer to submit
monitoring report for all the LGU’s funded projects or programs.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to reclassify the granting of livelihood
assistance to NGOs/POs treated as Donations into Due from Non-Government
Organizations/People’s Organizations account to properly monitor the
implementation of the projects.
We also recommended to require the recipients to prepare and submit a Fund
Utilization Report/Accomplishment Report prior to granting additional livelihood
assistance or taking up adjustments in the book to ensure that the fund is optimally
utilized.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Budget
Officer to strictly monitor the charges against the LDRRMF by maintaining a separate
Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) for QRF, MOOE and
CO and certify only items of expenditures that are within the appropriated amounts
based on the duly approved LDRRMFIP.
We also recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Budget Officer,
Municipal Accountant and the LDRRMO to submit legal basis for the use of fund
outside of the authorized amount and a revised and approved AIP for disbursement
of new expenditure items charged against the prior year’s unutilized balance.
Henceforth, the LGU must see to it that a revised AIP approved by the local
sanggunian and the LCE should be required for the release and use of unutilized
balances of the LDRRMF.

We recommended the Local Planning and Development Office spearheads the
setting up and maintenance of GAD Database by coordinating with DILG Field
Operation’s Officer and other concerned LGU officials. Further, we recommended the
GAD Focal Point System to: a) institutionalize the GAD database with adequate and
systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender
analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation; b) undertake gender
analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues and ensure that the programs,
projects and activities are responsive to the said issues, and c) revisit Annex B of PCW-
DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 as amended by JMC
2016-01, which identifies the data requirements for particular gender issues.
We recommended further that the GAD Accomplishment Report should
provide a breakdown of the expenditures for all GAD activities to facilitate review of
the fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a correct bank reconciliation between the book and the bank statement to reflect all reconciling items that require adjustments and correction in the book of accounts, and henceforth prepare and submit bank reconciliation statement regularly so that errors could be detected and corrected immediately.
observations.managementAction: Checks issued since 2016 and were still reported as outstanding beyond 2016 and still reported in 2018 bank reconciliation statement.
observations.reason: Checks were still reported as outstanding since 2016 and were still reported in the bank reconciliation.
